speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number seven, survivor and war crimes trial participant, Lawrence Rhee. And the date is April 9, 1996. What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, again, in Yugoslavia. These two persons had to dig their grave. They knelt in front of it. And behind them, the German soldier shoots one first, and then the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: And just state again how you got these pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, from the Yugoslav prosecution staff in the-- during the Nuremberg trials. Are you running short of time?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are pictures that Heinrich Hoffmann took of Hitler and his staff. And he gave them to me. They are all Heinrich Hoffman's photos. Here you see Hitler and Mussolini in Berchtesgaden.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is this the only copy that you know of that you have, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not aware of if it is the only one or not. I do not know. But it's a good one. Hitler in the Berghof. That's, again, an Heinrich Hoffman picture. And it's just a portrait. But the odd thing about this picture, and then the next one, is that when my son was stationed in Germany with the American Air Force, he picked up the next picture, what you see in a minute, and the two were taken quite apparently at the same time, the same necktie, the same desk. Everything is the same, except this is a huge blowup of a different pose, but taken at the same time. This is the-- at the same location, and Hitler sitting on the-- his desk there. But it is the same necktie, the same position of all the utensils and everything on the desk, except the different pose by Hitler. As I mentioned before, years and years later, my son, who was then stationed in Germany with the American Air Force, was able to buy this on the street. Again, just a pose by Hitler taken by Hoffmann. I have no idea where this is. Again, this is a picture by Hoffman of Hitler's Berghof in the Alps. The top one is as Hoffman saw it. In the next picture-- And the next picture you see here is the way I saw the same building.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken, actually, during-- no, shortly after the war. I, I took this shortly after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this Hitler's quarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where Hitler's-- where Hitler lived. Let's put it that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain that word.

speaker: interviewer
text: The word that you used for Hitler's quarters was called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Berghof. B-E-R-G-H-O-F-F. Double F? One F. And this is Hermann Goring before he came to power. He loved to hunt, and here is in his hunting dress. You can see the double-barreled rifle or shotgun and his hunting hat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken by?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken by Heinrich Hoffmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: And given to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Given to me, yes. Hitler and the Russian ambassador Molotov in Berlin at the time of the signing of the German-Russian Nonaggression Pact, which led directly to the invasion of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Molotov in that picture? In the middle or the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's an interpreter. Molotov in Berlin. Again, a Heinrich Hoffmann picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is he in the picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, which one is Molotov?

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Molotov is the-- let's call it the second one from the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is the man in the lighter uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's some SA [?  ?] or SS officer of some kind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is one of my favorites. This is von Ribbentrop, the German foreign minister, with Molotov, the Russian foreign minister, walking in Berlin under the Nazi and the Soviet flag.

speaker: interviewer
text: When do you think this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken at the time of the signing off the Nonaggression Pact between Russia and Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it is 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Hoffmann. Let me see if they-- if I-- this is just the reverse side of the previous picture. But it shows clearly that it is a copyright Presse Heinrich-- Presse Hoffmann, Heinrich Hoffmann. This is a picture of Hitler and Molotov together at the time of the signing of the Russian-German Nonaggression Pact. The man on the extreme left is an interpreter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's in the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Molotov.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hitler. This is a picture of Julius Streicher, the famous Jew baiter or infamous Jew baiter in-- I think that it was around 1923. He had been prisoner of the German authorities. And this was at the time of his release of the Nuremberg prison. And you can see the SA men saluting him and having a good time about his being-- having been released.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which one? The top picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: Top picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: The top picture is Bormann and Hitler selecting a wedding gift for somebody. I do not know for whom. Also Heinrich Hoffmann files. Again, Heinrich Hoffmann files. As we all know, after Italy lost the war, Mussolini was being-- had been taken prisoner by the English. But Hitler sent a German unit down there that liberated him. And this is the first picture after Mussolini's liberation, together with Hitler. You see a somewhat subdued Mussolini and a more radiant Hitler walking together.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in September, on September 13, 1943, and was taken in the German city of Rustenburg. This is a picture of Mussolini and Hitler together. It could be in the, in the Berghof. But I'm not sure on this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Berghof means what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Castle in the mountains, or a--

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are the other people in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some-- it looks like Jodl is in the back there, and Keitel. The one on the left-hand side, I do not know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Jodl and Keitel were who?

speaker: interviewee
text: The chief military commanders.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, yeah. Keitel and Mussolini before Italy's collapse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also part of Heinrich's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Heinrich Hoffmann's. They're all Heinrich Hoffmann's pictures. There is Hitler, Keitel, and Bormann looking over some kind of plans or maps. Again, Hoffmann files.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is who?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's who?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the left is Bormann, Hitler in the center, and Keitel on the right. The Swedish King Gustaf greets the Finnish foreign minister at the Stockholm airport in the conference of the Nordic states.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is who? Who's where?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right hand is the king. The left, the person in the center is the Finnish foreign minister, Kallio.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some aide. This is Admiral Raeder, just a portrait, taken by Hoffmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was Admiral Raeder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Admiral Raeder was-- I don't know who was in charge of it all, Donitz or Raeder. But he's one of the two chief Navy commanders in Germany, or was. King Boris, of Romania, visiting Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berchtes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Berchtesgaden, I believe. Not Berghof. Berchtesgaden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Berchtesgaden?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bavaria some place, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Or is it Berghof? Anyhow, may I-- well, I can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and where is the king of Romania? He's on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: King Boris, on the left side. Hitler and Monsignor Tiso. Tiso was a Catholic monsignor in Czechoslovakia. And he negotiated the surrender of Czechoslovakia to Hitler. Just why, I do not know. Again, it's a picture from the Hoffmann files. This is General von Witzleben. He was one of the persons who plotted against Hitler in his, in his attempt to take Hitler's life, on July 20th, I think it was, and was consequently executed by Hitler's forces. This is a picture of Hitler, the queen mother of Bulgaria, and her son, the crown prince Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just where it is, I don't know. But Hitler is receiving them as his guests.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is Michael in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Michael is the young fellow behind Hitler. Hitler is in the center of the picture. And to his right is the, the queen mother. This is Hitler to the left, Mannerheim of Finland in the center, and I believe it's, what I can see from here, it's Donitz on the right. He came to discuss strategy with the German high command.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Quisling leaving the quarters of Hitler. Quisling was the first real traitor amongst the German-- the nonaligned of nations in Europe. In other words, they were not for Hitler. But Quisling managed to betray his country. And ever since, all persons in Europe, at least during that period of time, who betrayed their country were called Quislings. Petain greeting Hitler in Montoire on October 25, 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, this picture was part of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, a picture a part of Hoffmann's collection. Petain was the French general who surrendered the French forces to Hitler's forces during World War II. His name spells P-E-T-A-I-N. And this is a picture of Laval, to the left, Hitler in the foreground right. Behind him, Goring. And in the very center, Galeazzo Ciano, who was Mussolini's son-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was Laval?

speaker: interviewee
text: Laval was the president of France. Again, a picture from the Hoffmann files. Shortly after Italy had been knocked out of the war, the Japanese ambassador came to visit Berlin. And here we have him on the left front row, the Japanese ambassador, and Goring. I do not know who the other people in this picture are.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Japanese ambassador, do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I do not. I'm sorry. The picture shows Hitler and Generalissimo Franco in Berlin, and marching-- walking along together. Franco never quite knew how to handle the situation. You see, he was a fascist, but he was not that extreme. And in fact, he helped hundreds, if not thousands, of refugees out of southern France and Germany to get out of their, out of their country. And this is a picture actually taken out of a movie. And in the movie strip, he shows that he didn't want to give the Hitler salute, so he just waved his hand up and down and up and down. And it looked really funny. This is, again, from that Hoffmann files. It's Goring and Count Galeazzo Ciano, who was Italy's foreign minister and also Mussolini's son-in-law. But apparently the two didn't get along too well, because Mussolini had him executed. This is a picture of Hitler to the front, in the front left, Goring standing up behind him, Jodl standing next to Goring, and General Keitel two persons behind him on the right. The reason I found this picture so interesting is that I-- that we see Hitler with glasses on. You see, very few people knew that Hitler needed glasses, because to the German people, he wanted to be the perfect human specimen. And there are very few, very few pictures that show him with glasses on. This is one of the few. Again, a Heinrich Hoffmann production. This shows Hitler not only with glasses on, but with a magnifying glass. This picture was taken at the time when Hitler was marking up Paris to be taken over. You can see the, the front lines of the German armies and the defending line of the German-- of the French armies defending the city. But this is Hitler making-- excuse me-- making the decisions what to do and telling his staff about them. But the remarkable thing is that Hitler not only needed glasses, he needed a magnifying glass. This is a picture at the time of the French surrender to the German forces. Here you see the German general staff and Goring and, of course, Hitler with a couple of bodyguards in there. And it is just a few seconds after this picture was taken that Hitler danced his famous jig. He was so overjoyed over the surrender of France that he actually danced. This is a picture, again, by Hoffmann, but by one of his helpers, because Hoffmann himself is in the picture. It's Hitler's 50th birthday, and Hoffman is giving him, as a gift, this present. The lady second from the left is Eva Braun. And the lady to the extreme right is Eva Braun's sister. And Heinrich Hoffmann is doing the explanation to Hitler of the painting. Again, Hoffmann collection, this is Goring with his pet lioness. And Hoffmann told me personally that when he left the room after taking these pictures, he had to change pants. He was so scared. This picture was actually used as photographic evidence. It shows Kaltenbrunner and Himmler, Himmler to the far left and Kaltenbrunner to the far right, in the concentration camp of Auschwitz. And Kaltenbrunner had always denied during the trials that he ever had been inside a concentration camp. He didn't know anything about them. But this placed him square in the middle of one. And thus, his fate was sealed. This album shows the pictures of the 24 defendants, these all pictures that Hoffmann took. But somebody in the American prosecution gave me these, this album.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was-- where was this given to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the Nuremberg trials.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know who gave it to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not recall. This is the first picture in the album. It shows Goring when he was really doing well. You see his distinguished flyer's medal, or whatever you call it, I don't know, hanging around his neck. And it was reported that Hoffmann took. This is one of the albums of photographic evidence that was given to me by the Russian prosecution. Colonel Petrovsky was the one who gave it to me. I mentioned to you, mentioned it earlier, that he gave me two albums. And this is one of-- containing pictures of cultural destruction. The other one that we'll see a little later contains the pictures of Auschwitz. Of the many pictures in the books, in this album, that show the cultural destruction that the Germans wreaked in Russia. This book, every picture in this book, is some, some destruction done to cultural treasures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this used for evidence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And this is a picture that shows me working with the Russian prosecution at some occasion. I forget the exact occasion. But you see me looking away from the camera, with the headphones on, on the right-hand side of the table, right behind the Russian prosecutor, who is speaking into the microphone at the speaker's desk. To the left of me is Colonel Petrovsky, the one who gave me the Russian evidence albums, or volumes, in my possession. This is a picture I took of Nuremberg during the time of the trials. You can see that the destruction was almost total. This is the other volume of the two volumes the Russians gave me. This shows more of the concentration camps and the suffering that the people there had to undergo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who actually took these pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians. I do not know who.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you when they took these pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time of liberation. All right. This is a picture that shows women's hair bagged in paper bags. The women in Auschwitz, before they were gassed, were shorn. Then their hair was packed in these paper bags that you see in the background there. And these bags were shipped back to Germany in order to make mattresses. This shot shows a part of a pyre in Camp Auschwitz. The amount of dead were so many, or were so big, that the crematoria could no longer take care of the bodies. So the Germans built pyres, a layer of wood, a layer of bodies, and a layer of wood, and so forth and so on. And this particular shot shows a pregnant woman in front that had first been shot and was partially, only partially burned, on the pyre. The lettering under the picture is in Russian. I got help from a Russian interpreter here in town, who translated for me what that meant. And I wrote it in pencil under there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to get close. Pregnant woman among executed by firing squad of Soviet citizens in Camp Klooga. I was mistaken. This is Camp Klooga, not in Finland. In Esland?

speaker: interviewer
text: Estonia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Estonia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was not in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was not in Auschwitz. I was mistaken on that.

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture was taken in a concentration camp in Estonia.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Klooga.

speaker: interviewer
text: Klooga?
